Thanks for pointing this out.

Numpy seems to have treated booleans differently than native Python, leading to unexpected behaviour. I assume that's the reason they included the depreciation version in newer numpy versions.

Python: 
```
>>> False - True
-1
```

Numpy:
```
>>> np.False_ - np.True_
np.True_
```
We'll change it for the next release.

You can easily remote targets using the following:

 * `(gdb) target extended-remote | gdbserver --multi stdio` (should provide full feature-parity with direct gdb usage - but note you'll need to pass several commands to actually get there)
 * `(gdb) target extended remote localhost:$PORT` (parse the port from the first output line of `gdbserver --multi localhost:0` - this is useful if you hit 100% CPU-usage hangs which I've occasionally seen)
 * `(gdb) target remote | gdbserver stdio $PROG $ARGS...` (a single-program experience, more typical for embedded remote targets)
 * `(gdb) target remote | gdbserver --attach stdio $PID`
 * `(gdb) target remote | vgdb --pid=$PID` (for programs started under `valgrind --vgdb=full --vgdb-errors=0`)

I don't think gdbgui currently handles this, but it is an important feature to be able to run *multiple* copies of GDB (not just multiple inferiors in a single GDB), and transparently manage all inferiors regardless of which GDB they're attached to. With twisted (see #65) this should be pretty easy.

from dtls import do_patch

do_patch()

ADDR = ('0.0.0.0', 1234)

sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
sock.bind(ADDR)
ssl_sock = ssl.wrap_socket(sock, ssl_version=ssl.PROTOCOL_DTLSv1_2,
                     server_side=True, certfile='cert.pem',

Hi,

Thanks for the reply! As far as I can tell gmpy2 is generally doing a pretty good job of handling subnormals.

Regarding `gmpy2.next_toward(x,y)`, that indeed works, but as far as I can tell it is an undocumented function, and it also shows the same bug.

Regarding the extra parameter, I would argue that `strict=True` is not the best choice because:
1. According to the C11 standard, an implementation of floating point is perfectly valid without support for subnormals, see 5.2.4.2.2 paragraph 3
2. The flag in the gmpy2 context is called `subnormalize`, so why not just do `subnormalize=True`?
3. gmpy2 differs from IEEE 754 the C11 standard and in a couple of other areas (and lack of support for subnormals is not one of them), so why not reserve that flag for actual deviations from IEEE and/or the C11 standard?

Examples of actual deviations from IEEE and C11:
1. In gmpy2 `invalid` is raised when one of the parameters is NaN; IEEE 754-2008 says you should not do that, see Section 6.2, start of last paragraph. (Applies to `next_toward()` and most if not all other functions in gmpy2).
2. The `nexttoward()` function does not follow the C11 standard section F.10.8.3.
3. Similarly, the `**` operator does not follow the requirements of the C11 standard section F.10.4.4.

I'm not arguing that gmpy2 should fix all the above deviations (although that would help me a lot!), but simply that the notion of a 'strict' mode should be reserved for such behavior differences.

I'd go for multiple `data` -tags in the `profile`, which have an optional `direction` -tag. Something like this:
```xml
  <telegram rorg="0xD5" type="1BS">
    <profiles description="Contacts and switches" func="0x00">
      <profile type="0x01">
        <data direction="1">
          <enum shortcut="CO" description="Contact" offset="7" size="1">
            <item description="open" value="0" />
            <item description="closed" value="1" />
          </enum>
        </data>
        <data direction="2">
          <enum shortcut="CO" description="Contact" offset="7" size="1">
            <item description="open" value="0" />
            <item description="closed" value="1" />
          </enum>
        </data>
      </profile>
    </profiles>
  </telegram>
```

This way, in case there's only one `data` declaration, we can use it both for transmit and receive. In case there are multiple, we can choose based on the direction. 

Also, the incoming message parsing should always be first in the XML, for consistency. I haven't checked the documentation more thoroughly, but I assume it's this way in there also. If not, we still have the `direction` attribute to follow, no matter what.

Hi,

Let me start by explaining why some design decisions were taken for ``isbnlib``.

Probably, by number, the main users of ``isbnlib`` are people (teachers, students, researchers, …) who use the package ``isbntools`` (or other more advanced bibliographic software) to manage small bibliographies. 

To serve this target,  ``isbnlib`` focused on three main features:
- ISBN validation, extraction and transformation.
- Bibliographic metadata (ISBN, Title, Authors, Publisher,  Year).
- Record formatters for bibliographic databases (BibTeX, Word, EndNote, …). 

Later, to complement these, some features to handle DOI references, book summary and  image covers, were added too.

For the selection of metadata fields,  the main criteria was to use fields of the Dublin Core for which the main data sources (xISBN, Gooble Books,  Open Library) provided good data quality. Some fields like Language and Price were left out because didn’t conform with these criteria. This explains the *canonical fields*:  ISBN, Title, Authors, Publisher and  Year.

One other important goal was that the library would handle any reference independent of the language. So for default metadata provider was selected xISBN, that has metadata for books from over the world. However, other providers were included (Google Books, Open Library and isbndb.com) and the possibility to add other sources by the users was always there.

Now I made it easier to add extensions with *addins*: 
- Metadata providers
- Bibliographic formatters

Metadata addins,  need to implement a function ``query(isbn)`` that returns a dictionary with the canonical data (ISBN, Title, Authors, Publisher, Year). That all is need!

The builtin addins follow the pattern: (1) search with isbn, (2) get page (html, json, xml, …), (3) extract/parse the main data elements (4) Map and validate the data as canonical. However this is not mandatory, all is need is the above mentioned function.

So, my proposal is to implement this function with minimum requirements for *librairie-de-paris.fr* as a start.

What do you think?

**P.S.** As far as I know, "webscraping" is ‘fair use’ in EU (but many sites make it difficult or practically impossible to scrap main info), anyway an explicit warning must be added to the code. I don’t know what the situation is for other parts of the world.

Hi @kontez! Sorry for the slow response.

The exclude syntax is always a regex and it should match the full URL, so as an example, if I had a vulnerable parameter `foo` on example.com, the regex would look like this:
```
zap-cli exclude "http://example\.com/\?foo=.*"
```

So, for example, here's some output from a test run where I had just mapped example.com to my localhost with a vulnerable site:
```
$ zap-cli quick-scan -s xss "http://example.com/index.php?foo=bar"
[INFO]            Running a quick scan for http://example.com/index.php?foo=bar
[INFO]            Issues found: 1
+----------------------------------+--------+----------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Alert                            | Risk   |   CWE ID | URL                                                                                          |
+==================================+========+==========+==============================================================================================+
| Cross Site Scripting (Reflected) | High   |       79 | http://example.com/index.php?foo=%22%3E%3Cscript%3Ealert%281%29%3B%3C%2Fscript%3E |
+----------------------------------+--------+----------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
$ zap-cli session new
$ zap-cli quick-scan -s xss -e "http://example\.com/index\.php\?foo=.*" "http://example.com/index.php?foo=bar"
[INFO]            Running a quick scan for http://example.com/index.php?foo=bar
[ERROR]           Error running active scan: "URL Not Found in the Scan Tree". Make sure the URL is in the site tree by using the open-url or scanner commands before running an active scan.
$ zap-cli session new
$ zap-cli quick-scan -s xss -e "http://example\.com/.*" "http://example.com/index.php?foo=bar"
[INFO]            Running a quick scan for http://example.com/index.php?foo=bar
[ERROR]           Error running active scan: "URL Not Found in the Scan Tree". Make sure the URL is in the site tree by using the open-url or scanner commands before running an active scan.
```

If it was already in the site tree, then the scan wouldn't error, but would just return empty results. Note that this won't stop reporting an alert that was already found, it only prevents the active scanner attacking URLs that match the regex. If there's already an alert raised from before you added the regex, you have to remove the alert from the session (or start a new session).

Can you provide an example regex that doesn't work (maybe on a test site, so it's unrelated to your work)?

Although, I've had issues with cmake not propagating target dependencies when it compiles cuda executables.  So you might have to just add the `include_directories` statement yourself.  You also want to make sure you pass the compiler options to nvcc as well, which you can get from cmake's target options with cmake code like this:

```
get_property(dlib_opts TARGET dlib::dlib PROPERTY COMPILE_OPTIONS)
list(APPEND CUDA_NVCC_FLAGS "${dlib_opts}")
```

Presumably some newer version of cmake will have cuda tooling that doesn't mess this up or does it in some smarter way.

It's awesome that you ask this, it's actually been on my mind lately. There is still a lot to consider, but I think there is a way to make work, maybe I will get around to it as early as this weekend.

If you're interested, I'll describe briefly what's going on. We originally chose to only support index based assignment because it is a very specific case that we thought would be easy for people to refactor their code around. Index based assignment in Python can be transformed to a functionally identical function call to `__setitem__`, making it a subset of this broader category of function calls. Deco currently just defers index based assignment to happen at the next synchronization event, which happens the first time a mutated variable is encountered in a read context. This is where things get complicated, or rather why index based mutation is simpler to handle. Function calls are complicated to reason about because they can mutate extra variables, which could then require a synchronization even before they're read. So now we would need to implement some much more complicated data flow analysis, _or_ make some restrictions on what kind of function calls we allow. Deco was sort of born out of this dichotomy in the first place, it's partially motivated by some earlier work called [Pydron](http://pydron.org/). They designed their system around generality, it has some crazy complicated data flow analysis and it has seen some academic success I think. We designed deco around simplicity and the common case, which allowed us to cut a lot of corners and I think will allow it to eventually become a production ready tool. I think deco will be sort of stuck with this simplicity of generality philosophy, but I also think there may be a way to stay simple and support what you're asking about.

**TL;DR** version is that it's really hard to do this in a general way, but probably there are some reasonable restrictions we can apply and implement something that works for the common cases.

I'm going to leave this issue open until we can make some decision, either on how to appropriately handle deferring function calls, or to not handle them and instead throw an exception.

Kinda expected - you'd see better performance on a RPi model 3B than a PiZero (obviously), and remember its having to push out greyscale-converted 256*64 pixels per frame for video. 

For simpler drawing tasks where only parts of the frame changes, then it does a "diff-to-previous" to only draw the changed areas - this helps improve performance significantly (but not for video where there is a lot of churn between frames).

----
The main intent for this library is a simple and accessible drawing abstraction (without too many dependencies) rather than high frame-rate video rendering. I've just implemented a driver for SSD1351 (128x128 full color, see #174) and handling that many pixels sequentially is getting to the limit of what is achievable in python.

Maybe a future direction might be to look at rewriting the display loop rendering in C with python FFI...

I think it's not too hard to get velocities into TreeCorr as a quantity to be correlated.  Right now we just have counts (N) scalars (K) and spinors (G), since these are the things we normally need in weak lensing and large scale structure.

But I've thought some about adding vectors, for which I'd suggest the indicator letter V.  In 2D, these would work a lot like spinors, but with exp(i phi) rotation rather than exp(2i phi).  And I think once you project the two vectors relative to the line joining them, the quantities you care about are

    xi+ = v1x * v2x + v1y * v2y
    xi- = v1x * v2x - v1y * v2y

much like xi+, xi- for GG correlations.  There are also two cross terms, which in the complex formulation come out as the imaginary parts of the above quantities:

    xi+_im = v1x * v2y + v1y * v2x
    xi-_im = v1x * v2y - v1y * v2x

Both of these latter two numbers have expectation zero under normal symmetry assumptions, so they are usually not particularly interesting.

For 3D vectors, I'm not sure, but by analogy, I'd imagine that you'd probably care about

    xi+ = v1x * v2x + v1y * v2y + v1z * v2z

After that I guess there are 8 others, but I'm not sure which linear combinations are interesting.  Probably most of them have expectation zero under reasonable symmetry assumptions.

So is this the kind of thing you were thinking about?  Correlate V with V, N with V, and N with N?  

Or did you really mean that you wanted the underlying metric to include the velocities?  i.e. just NN correlations, but where distances were in 6 dimensions somehow?  This latter scenario seems problematic to me, since I'm not sure how to calculate a single "distance" for two objects that are separated by 15 Mpc in space and have delta V = 230 km/s.  

It seems to me that it would be more useful to include the velocity as a thing to be correlated rather than as part of the metric.

> __all__ is defined as a list, this means pep257 cannot reliably detect contents

I believe those are just the strange, corner-case rantings of whoever wrote the pep257 tool. Almost all packages use lists for `__all__`, including Sphinx, six, Jinja2, nose, pip, Pygments, and even the examples in https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/modules.html itself. The `pep257` tool is welcome to assume `__all__` is immutable and should do so quietly. It would be exceedingly weird to mutate it and isn't worth a startling warning.

> I could just as easily have adopted the six package, but I wanted as few external dependencies as possible.

The neat thing about six is that it's a single file, designed to be embedded. We could do that if the dependency bothers you, or we could just factor out our own 2/3 fixes. We have lots of choices.

The issue is that the 2.3.2 release uses a new feature of `calibre.gui2.ProceedQuestion` in the `__call__` method: `log_viewer_unique_name`. This keyword-argument is not available in Calibre API before version 2.5.7 and was [contributed by  JimmXinu recently](https://github.com/kovidgoyal/calibre/commit/463603dab0ac8cf169e1199bb41ad63599cbcb16).

There are two ways around this: Either remove usage of this new feature entirely or make it dependent on the used Calibre version.

I agree this is problematic. I'm working on some things that should improve matters for shrinking performance.

Some notes:

* The timeout does apply to the shrinking stage. I'm not sure why you're seeing it taking 10 minutes. That might be a bug. I'd be interested in an example where this happens if you can share.
* Even if it's not, what you're experiencing is very atypically slow. Some of the problem here is probably the recursive example, but even then it's very rare for it to be that slow. The normal max_shrinks is rarely a problem for most examples.
* It's almost impossible to show things to the user prior to shrinking due to the way Python's test frameworks work. 

I don't think it's very likely, but one possibility is that sometimes new bugs occur during shrinking, and it might be that one of those bugs is triggering an infinite loop in your own code. The timeout is soft, so if an individual test never returns or takes a very long time to return it won't be terminated prematurely.

One thing you could try is set the environment variable HYPOTHESIS_VERBOSITY_LEVEL=verbose to see what it's doing (you'll need to turn off output capturing in py.test or nose as well)

The _cut_ method will return a new _MDF_ with all the channels cut according to the _start_, _stop_ and _whence_ arguments. This means that you don't have to specify channels. In your example 
``` python
mdf.cut(start=300)
```
the cut is done starting from timestamp=300s to the end of measurement. If you get an empty file then you should check if there are any samples beyond 300s.

You can chain _cut_ and _filter_ to get a subset of channels
```python
signals = ["s1", "s2", "s3]
new_mdf = mdf.filter(signals).cut(start=300)
```

Have a look at the _cut_ documentation here http://asammdf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/mdf.html. To cut, for example, from the start of the measurement and up to 5s relative to the start of measurement you can write
```python
mdf.cut(stop=5, whence=1)
```

On 32bit Python your only bet is to use _load_measured_data=False_

Couldn't you make the target function of the schedule job into a coroutine function? The asyncio executor handles plain functions via threads due to historical reasons.

> Can you elaborate on this?

Let's say I have 5 nodes and I deployed APScheduler on all of them. I use a memory jobstore and threaded executors. I have idempotent jobs so that I can execute them whenever I want on any of my nodes. I run a job every 10 minutes using an `IntervalTrigger` or a `CronJob`. This job calls an external API + performs some CPU intensive tasks.

- I don't want the loss of one node to compromise the health of the cluster.
- On average, I don't want my nodes to perform the API call at the exact same moment. This would cause a DoS.
- If there's a lock to take for example, I don't want the same node to take it and do all the work everytime. I want the work to be roughly evenly balanced across nodes. (e.g. If one node is unable to do the job for any reason, I want to be informed ASAP + in the case of CPU intensive operations, it's generally a good idea to balance load => CPU credit)
- I want to minimize the number of times I execute the same job on many nodes in the same time interval. It's generally pointless.

The "lean" way to achieve this (I think) is to add a random offset to the job execution (as suggested in this issue) and randomize whether the job executes or not. It's cheap yet good-enough HA and LB without involving a master election, a distributed cron system, a service discovery or such complex and expensive things.

But general case: you know how many sysadmins like to run their cron jobs at fixed non-sharp hours to "respect resources". Having a random offset is just a clean way to address this issue and it's probably what most people here are willing to see.

@skynyrd Thanks for taking the time to file this issue report.
I see the problem, and it is due to my bad porting of the example files from Flask-CORS to Sanic-CORS.

The problem is that by default Sanic accepts only 'GET' requests for all routes. That means all other request types including 'HEAD','POST', 'PUT','DELETE' and 'OPTIONS' return a 405 "Method not allowed" response.

Flask-CORS has a config option called 'Automatic Options' (which is `True` by default) that handles 'OPTIONS' requests automatically. This was ported to Sanic-CORS , however this is less useful in Sanic because the sanic router will reject the request with 'Method not allowed' before invoking the route, so sanic-cors cannot handle the OPTIONS automatically.

You can fix your example in one of a few ways.
1) Change the sanic route decorator to the following:
`@app.route('/timeline', methods=['GET', 'OPTIONS'])`
Now your example will work correctly, CORS headers will be added, but it will return the same content as the 'GET' request when requesting 'OPTIONS'.
2) Same as `1)` above but return an empty response on an 'OPTIONS' request. (CORS headers will still be applied). Like so:
```
@app.route('/timeline', methods=['GET', 'OPTIONS'])
async def get_timeline(request, user):
    if request.method == "OPTIONS":
        return HTTPResponse()
    ...
```
3) Use the `cross_origin` decorator. The `cross_origin` decorator will do the same as `2)` automatically so you can do this:
```
@app.route('/timeline', methods=["GET", "OPTIONS"])
@cross_origin(app)
async def get_timeline(request, user):
    ...
```

I have updated the `app_based_example` and `blueprints_based_example` example files so that they work, using method `1)` above.

I am currently working an a new release that fixes this whole issue by using a sanic request middleware that intercepts the request before passing it to the sanic router, to do _real_ automatic 'OPTIONS' handling, however this breaks functional-equivalence with flask-CORS. This feature will be in the next release. 

For interest's sake, I tested this example using blueprints too, and with blueprints the problem is different, and more severe.
In the last update I merged a pull-request from a user which adds exception-handling support, so CORS headers will be still applied when Sanic responds with an exceptions.

It turns out that this addition broke CORS on blueprints, so Sanic was raising an exception when registering CORS on a blueprint, and causing the whole app to not work. I have pushed a fix for this too, and it will also be in the next release.

@sstarcher The solution here is really a wrapper around awslimitchecker, not integrating Prometheus.

For the purpose of #152, the solution needs work within the awslimitchecker public API, i.e. it needs to be instantiated inside the AwsLimitChecker class.

I'd be happy to merge this as an example script in the ``docs/examples/`` directory, if you can include full test coverage for it and make the http server port configurable. However, incorporation of a metrics solution needs to be something that is inside awslimitchecker itself, and can be enabled and configured via either the existing CLI interface or via arguments to the AwsLimitChecker() class, as described in my [comment on 152](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/issues/152#issuecomment-277992950).

Keep in mind that AwsLimitChecker is only capable of checking one region at a time. Looking at the example code here, if we could simply run prometheus during each individual run (one region), I do feel that this could fit within the current AwsLimitChecker paradigm. We'd simply need to instantiate the metrics service class before doing anything else (this would start the prometheus server, and do whatever other setup is needed) and then have the metrics service class expose methods like ``before_find_usage()``, ``find_usage_finished(limits)``, etc.

If we're adding metrics support to awslimitchecker, at a minimum, I'd like it to be something that will work with statsd and DataDog. I think that a generic solution with a class that's instantiated before any usage/limits are checked, and then has methods that are called at various points in the process, should suffice for most use cases that I'm aware of.

Okay, so I wrote up a quick example using `requests`. Below is the code and the output on my machine. Notice how it behaves when you pass in `None`. It seems to be defaulting to either TLSv1_1 or TLSv1_2, neither of which are supported by the first server.

```python
import ssl
import requests
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter
from requests.packages.urllib3.poolmanager import PoolManager

class SSLAdapter(HTTPAdapter):
    '''An HTTPS Transport Adapter that uses an arbitrary SSL version.'''
    def __init__(self, ssl_version=None, **kwargs):
        self.ssl_version = ssl_version

        super(SSLAdapter, self).__init__(**kwargs)

    def init_poolmanager(self, connections, maxsize, block=False):
        self.poolmanager = PoolManager(num_pools=connections,
                                       maxsize=maxsize,
                                       block=block,
                                       ssl_version=self.ssl_version)

ssl_versions = {
    #ssl.PROTOCOL_SSLv2: 'SSLv2', # Removed in 3.6
    ssl.PROTOCOL_SSLv3: 'SSLv3',
    ssl.PROTOCOL_SSLv23: 'SSLv23',
    ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1: 'TLSv1',
    ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1_1: 'TLSv1_1',
    ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1_2: 'TLSv1_2',
    #ssl.PROTOCOL_TLS, # New in 3.6,
    None: 'None'
}

urls = [
    'https://webmail.leedsworld.com/',
    'https://www.google.com',
    'https://www.outlook.com'
]

for url in urls:
    print(url)
    for k,v in ssl_versions.items():
        s = requests.Session()
        s.mount('https://', SSLAdapter(ssl_version=k))
        try:
            s.get(url)
            print ('\t{}\tSuccess'.format(v))
        except:
            print ('\t{}\tFail'.format(v))

Hi @jakeo, thank you for reporting,

So I just analyzed the issue, the problem is that **ssh** will always call set the "%p" in the `ProxyCommand`, so if you do not add a `-p 12345` in your cmdline, it will pass `%p=22` by default.

---

Here, a quick demo:

```console
$ assh proxy vm-5 --dry-run
FATA[0000] Proxy error: dry-run: Golang native TCP connection to '192.168.1.155:2222'
$ ssh -v vm-5
...
INFO[0000] Host vm-5
INFO[0000]   Port 22
INFO[0000]   # HostName: 192.168.1.155
...
```

---

I just opened a new issue (#122) to fix this.

---

For now, I suggest you to use one of these alternatives:

* Switch to this configuration file:

    ```yaml
      vm-[0-9]:
        ProxyCommand: nc 192.168.1.155 2222
    ```

    It keeps your advanced regex and matches with your example, but it won't support additional fields (`IdentityFile` for instance).

    or
* switch from `vm-[0-9]` to `vm-*` if you can use more simple regex, this way you will have full **ssh** options support

Hi !

This is because you're doing it wrong.

Given your case, you have two choices:
- Use the request parser to declare form parameters and give it as argument to your decorator: http://flask-restplus.readthedocs.org/en/stable/documenting.html#input-and-output-models.

  This is the recommended way because it performs validation (and expose Swagger documentation)
  ```python
# First choice: RequestParser
parser = api.parser()
parser.add_argument('resource_name', type=str, help='Resource name', location='form')
parser.add_argument('attribute_name', type=str, help='Attribute name', location='form')
parser.add_argument('resource_value', type=str, help='Attribute value', location='form')
parser.add_argument('config_name', type=str, help='Config name', location='form')

@api.route(BASE_URL+'/update_resource_attribute')
class UpdateResourceAttributeResource(Resource):
    @api.doc(parser=parser)
    def post(self):
        args = parser.parse_args()
        # Do whatever you want with parsed/validated arguments
        resource_name = args['resource_name']
        # ...
```

- Use the right syntax for declaring parameters
  ```python
# Second choice: simple parameters declaration
@api.route(BASE_URL+'/update_resource_attribute')
class UpdateResourceAttributeResource(Resource):

    @api.doc(params={
        'resource_name': {'in': 'form', description': 'Resource name'},
        'attribute_name': {'in': 'form', description': 'Attribu}te name'},
        'attribute_value': {'in': 'form', description': 'Attribute value'},
        'config_name': {'in': 'form', description': 'Config name'})
    def post(self):
        resource_name = request.form['resource_name']
        attribute_name = request.form['attribute_name']
        attribute_value = request.form['attribute_value']
        config_name = request.form['config_name']
```

I took a *brief* look at what it would take to migrate from `ctypes` to using the `cffi.api.FFI` object exposed by [pyca/cryptography](/pyca/cryptography), and the migration appears nontrivial. `cffi` does have some nice convenience features like attaching native destructors to be invoked during garbage collection, e.g.:

```py
new_bn = ffi.gc(_ssl.BN_new(), _ssl.BN_free)
```

However, this project makes a *lot* of calls out to the SSL library (thankfully, they're all in [`key.py`](/petertodd/python-bitcoinlib/blob/master/bitcoin/core/key.py) as far as I can tell). Every call to `_ssl.[...]` would have to be rewritten (or at least reexamined and tested). That's not to say it's a bad idea, just that it was more work than I had originally hoped. (Wishful thinking, I know.) :blush:

@pmarti28 the error is the same, but your problem is different, I think.

In your case, there actually is a legit simultaneous access of a handle by two or more threads, because you are emitting with the same `socketio` object from multiple green threads, and this object keeps in its state the file handle on which the writes are made.

Your solution removes the stored file handle, and creates a new one each time a write is made. Clearly this addresses the problem, but I'm concerned about the performance cost of constantly having to open new handles.

As a short term solution I think your change is fine, but I'm not sure how I feel about penalizing the majority of users who do not have a set up like yours.

An alternative solution that I have used, is to not run celery under eventlet. In my opinion there is no reason to switch Celery to eventlet, in general Celery works better with a pool of process workers of a fixed size. Running Celery w/o eventlet will address this problem, because each worker process will have a separate instance of `socketio`. If you try this, in addition to disabling eventlet support in the Celery config, make sure you create the `socketio` instance with `async_mode='threading'`, which effectively disables eventlet in the socketio subsystem.

A long term solution for this problem is, I think, something that works similarly to context variables in Flask would make sense. That would allow a different file handle to be kept for each thread, all transparently done for you.

1. A `nodeRDD` represents all the nodes in a TF cluster, whereas `workerRDD` only represents the workers.  The reason we do this is because the PS nodes usually just run forever in TF.  So in our design, they would continue to "occupy" a Spark task slot, so the remaining slots would be "completed" workers.  On any given worker, the Spark executor should have a consistent "working directory".  Again, note that we require one executor == one task == one TF worker.
2. This is mostly due to the InputMode.SPARK.  In that mode, it hard to tell if the end of a partition also signifies the end of the entire dataset, so we needed to send an EOF signal to all the input queues to tell the TF process that there's no more data arriving. 
3. It might be possible to just run the PS node as a thread in the executor's python worker.  My main motivation for using a subprocess was to try to avoid any issues with the global interpreter lock (between Spark and TensorFlow "processes").

Note: I'm still not absolutely happy with the shutdown sequence, so I'd like to revisit it someday (when time allows).  The main challenge is shutting down the cluster cleanly while also handling error conditions and retries.

@btallman 

I suggest adding 2 new configuration vars to airflow.cfg:

- `hide_paused_dags` (defaulted to False)
  * This determines whether the DAG Overview page hides DAGs by default. For your org, you may strongly consider setting it to True since you'd like to unclutter your UI.
- `enable_show_hide_paused_dags` (defaulted to False)
  * This determines whether your new feature is enabled/disabled. By default, it will be disabled and the expectation is that the DAG Overview page will look the same as it does today

At Agari, we sometimes pause a DAG and then forget to re-enable it. Hiding paused DAGs would make it easier for us to forget to re-enable them. However, I believe this feature is useful enough to allow merging given an additional configuration parameters.

Another thing I think you need to think about is that your paused dags are still getting parsed. The new scheduler launches a subprocess to parse each dag file.  You can look at `process_file` in `jobs.py`.

Regarding the difference between `self.service.tables().insert` and `self.service.jobs().insert`:

I couldn't create an external table with `self.service.jobs().insert`. It can be done by using `configuration.query.tableDefinitions` but in that you need to specify the query as well in the query parameter.

So in that case, I will be able to create an external table only if I want to query it immediately.

> By the way, is there a reason in setup.py#L1: from distutils.core import setup to prefer distutils over setuptools?

For historical reasons, largely having to do with the fact that setuptools does unacceptable egg-based installs by default, and corrupts sys.path as well, causing a decade or so of packaging nightmares. With setuptools (only starting with version 25) and pip, we are considering moving to use setuptools by default, only disabling the implicit building of eggs that should never happen. That said, since `pip install .` is the only way any Python package should ever be installed (`python setup.py install` should never by typed again), whether we import setuptools or not in setup.py doesn't make much difference.

I think this is a pretty clever solution.  Regarding base64 encoding, the only downside to not doing that now is that this will almost always require the user to properly quote the value:

```
$ aws s3api list-objects --bucket bucket --max-items 2 --region us-west-2 --output text
...
NEXTTOKEN	[None, '2']

$ aws s3api list-objects --bucket bucket --max-items 2 --region us-west-2 --output text \
        --starting-token "[None, '2']"
                         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^
```

Users should be comfortable with quoting things, but the base64 encoding means there will be one less way to enter an incorrect command.

I found the reason for the error and have 3 good news and a bad one.

**Good**:
* Trigger for the error is very specific
* workaround is easy
* I have code to reproduce it :)

**Bad**:
* I have no idea, where the reason for this error is

**Requirements to trigger the error**
1. A folder name of the submodule's path must be part of submodules name 
   Example: path="/my/sub/module/" and name="modu"
2. git Repo was pulled and not cloned (if cloned only, bug doesn't appear)

**Example Code**
You need an (empty) git repo with pull privileges.

```python

import git
import os
import shutil
from urllib.parse import quote


USERNAME = "USERNAME"
PASSWORD = quote("PASSWORD", safe="")

GIT_MAIN_URL = "https://%s:%s@github.com/danwos/gitpython_test.git" % (USERNAME, PASSWORD)
GIT_SUBMODULE_URL = "https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython"

GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "repo_temp")

AUTHOR = git.Actor("USER", "EMAIL")

class RepoTest:
    def __init__(self):
        self.repo = None
        self.submodule = None

    def runt_test(self):
        #############################################################################
        # LOOP 1
        # This will work
        #############################################################################
        sm_name = "source"
        sm_path = "test/source/"
        print("\nLOOP 1 - name: %s - path: %s (sm initial created)" % (sm_name, sm_path))

        self._clone_repo()

        # Will delete all existing submodules and push the change.
        self._clean_submodule()
        self._commit_changes("Cleaned submodule")

        self._create_submodule(sm_name, os.path.join(GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE, sm_path))
        self._commit_changes("Added submodule")
        self._print_info()

        #############################################################################
        # LOOP 2 (same as loop 1)
        # This will NOT work
        # trigger for error: folder_name of path == name
        # and "git pull" instead of a fresh clone
        #############################################################################
        sm_name = "source"
        sm_path = "test/source/"
        print("\nLOOP 2 - name: %s - path: %s (sm from main repo)" % (sm_name, sm_path))
        self._pull_repo()
        self._create_submodule(sm_name, os.path.join(GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE, sm_path))
        self._commit_changes("Added submodule")
        self._print_info()

        #############################################################################
        # LOOP 3
        # This will work
        #############################################################################
        sm_name = "source_2_1_0"
        sm_path = "test/source_2/"
        print("\nLOOP 3 - name: %s - path: %s (sm initial created)" % (sm_name, sm_path))
        self._pull_repo()
        self._create_submodule(sm_name, os.path.join(GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE, sm_path))
        self._commit_changes("Added submodule")
        self._print_info()

        #############################################################################
        # LOOP 4 (Same as loop 3)
        # This will NOT work
        # trigger for error: folder_name of path in name
        #############################################################################
        sm_name = "source_2_1_0"
        sm_path = "test/source_2/"
        print("\nLOOP 4 - name: %s - path: %s (sm from main repo)" % (sm_name, sm_path))
        self._pull_repo()
        self._create_submodule(sm_name, os.path.join(GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE, sm_path))
        self._commit_changes("Added submodule")
        self._print_info()

        #############################################################################
        # LOOP 5
        # This will work!!!!
        #############################################################################
        sm_name = "awesome"
        sm_path = "test/temp/"
        print("\nLOOP 5 - name: %s - path: %s (sm initial created)" % (sm_name, sm_path))
        self._pull_repo()
        self._create_submodule(sm_name, os.path.join(GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE, sm_path))
        self._commit_changes("Added submodule")
        self._print_info()

        #############################################################################
        # LOOP 6
        # This will NOT work!!!!  (Why, thought it should... :( )
        #############################################################################
        sm_name = "awesome"
        sm_path = "test/temp/"
        print("\nLOOP 6 - name: %s - path: %s (sm from main repo)" % (sm_name, sm_path))
        self._pull_repo()
        self._create_submodule(sm_name, os.path.join(GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE, sm_path))
        self._commit_changes("Added submodule")
        self._print_info()

    def _clone_repo(self):
        self.repo = git.Repo.clone_from(GIT_MAIN_URL, GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE)

    def _pull_repo(self):
        self.repo = git.Repo(GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE)
        self.repo.remotes.origin.pull()

    def _clean_submodule(self):
        for sm in self.repo.submodules:
            sm.remove()

    def _create_submodule(self, name, path):
        self.submodule = self.repo.create_submodule(name=name,
                                                    path=path,
                                                    url=GIT_SUBMODULE_URL)

    def _commit_changes(self, message=""):
        self.repo.index.commit(message, author=AUTHOR, committer=AUTHOR)
        self.repo.remotes.origin.push()

    def _print_info(self):

        try:
            print("Name: ", self.submodule.name)
        except AttributeError:
            print("submodule.name raised AttributeError")

        try:
            print("URL: ", self.submodule.url)
        except AttributeError:
            print("submodule.url raised AttributeError")

        try:
            self.submodule.update()
            print("submodule updated")
        except AttributeError:
            print("submodule.update() raised AttributeError")


if "main" in __name__:
    if os.path.exists(GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE):
        shutil.rmtree(GIT_MAIN_WORKSPACE)
    test = RepoTest()
    test.runt_test()

I think this is probably a RHEL/CentOS/SELinux feature but I haven’t tested it anywhere so it could just be universally true. I wouldn’t generally recommend logging in as root in the first place, you won’t encounter this if you login as the target user to start with. `su` is not perfect and it is usually recommended to do things with sudo

PACKAGE=cartridge-downloads  # Change this to test other packages

pip install 'setuptools==34.4.1'
pip install --no-binary "$PACKAGE" "$PACKAGE"
pip show -f "$PACKAGE" >good-info

pip uninstall "$PACKAGE"

pip install 'setuptools==35.0.0'
pip install --no-binary "$PACKAGE" "$PACKAGE"
pip show -f "$PACKAGE" >bad-info

diff -u good-info bad-info
```

(Note that this uses `pip install --no-binary` to install the sdist rather than the wheel.)

@rueberger per the [docs](http://pykafka.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/simpleconsumer.html#pykafka.simpleconsumer.SimpleConsumer.reset_offsets), `reset_offsets` doesn't require a special type, just a sequence of `(pykafka.partition.Partition, int)` tuples. As noted in https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/issues/830, the internal logic of `reset_offsets` very likely needs to be updated, or at the very least evaluated for correctness. I agree that treating the given integers as timestamps before offsets is a little weird, and I'm going to see if there's a way around this given my recently deepened understanding of the Offsets API.

If memory serves, the timestamp information for a given message should be available via `Message.timestamp` if the Kafka version being used supports it.

I agree your producer cannot produce `None` I tried it. In my case producer is different and consumer is from pykafka. But I still think it must handle `None` messages, I assume compaction process periodic not real time and we cannot wait for compaction process to delete null payloads.

@messense the simple answer is to `stop()` and `start()` the consumer in repsonse to these errors. This is definitely suboptimal and isn't guaranteed to make the issue go away, but it will at least unstick your message consumption for some amount of time. The optimal fix is, of course, to get to the bottom of why the `ManagedBalancedConsumer` doesn't handle node failures properly.

        client = APIClient(enforce_csrf_checks=True)

        response = client.post('/auth-token/', self.data, format='json')

        decoded_payload = utils.jwt_decode_handler(response.data['token'])

        self.assertEqual(response.status_code, status.HTTP_200_OK)
        self.assertEqual(decoded_payload['username'], self.username)
        self.assertEqual(response.data['user'], self.user)
```

Here is my proposal for the structure of a truth catalog output type from the config processing:
```
output : 
    truth :
        file_name : truth.fits
        columns :
            num : obj_num
            g1 : gal.shear.g1
            g2 : gal.shear.g2
            x : image_posx
            y : image_posy
            psf_e1 : 
                type : Eval
                str : 'psf_e * math.cos(psf_beta.rad())`
                fpsf_e : { type : Current, key : psf.ellip.e }
                apsf_beta : { type : Current, key : psf.ellip.beta }
            psf_e2 : 
                type : Eval
                str : 'psf_e * math.sin(psf_beta.rad())`
                fpsf_e : { type : Current, key : psf.ellip.e }
                apsf_beta : { type : Current, key : psf.ellip.beta }
```
The `columns` item is a dict whose keys will be the names of the columns in the output file, and the values are the values in each row for that column.  Typically, the value is taken to be the "Current" value for that item in the config dict.  i.e. it works just like the `type : Current` value type.  So these would look like `gal.shear.g1` and `gal.shear.g2` above.

However, I think it also makes sense to allow some special values that config knows about for each object.  These are the same items that are allowed to be in `Eval` items.  In the above example, these include `obj_num`, `image_posx`, and `image_posy`.

And finally, it's probably worth allowing calculated values here too.  In the above example, we convert from an EBeta specification of the PSF ellipticity to e1,e2.  I'm not sure how useful this really is, so I'd be ok with not including this option.  But I think it would be easy to allow.

Comments welcome.  I'll probably be doing the coding for this issue in the #691 branch, since it is related to things I'll be doing for that.  But discussion about this feature can be kept here.

Cosimo contributed quite a lot of time to resolving my issues. I now have a working prototype.  I learned of lot of background info, but had a couple of specific take-aways.
1) the most appropriate setup() keyword for installing executable programs is 'scripts'. 'data_files' is pretty much deprecated, and produces different results with different distribution formats. Use of 'scripts' may not be stable over time, as it is somewhat old - use of the entry_points keyword arg is encouraged instead - and was not intended for use files that were not Python scripts. 
2)  For data files that will end up in the distribution, it is better to not use package_data, and instead to use the MANIFEST.in file with the setup() keyword 'include_package_data=True'.
3) When the package is compressed with the zip file format ( default with egg and wheels), relative paths to data files won't work. You can force the package to NOT be zipped with  with setup() keyword ' zip_safe=False,', although Python will do this automatically if it notices references to '_file__' or Python extension files.  You can use the module 'package_utils' to safely load stuff from within a zip file.
4) Be wary of versions of pip left over from other installations of python on your system. The safest way to to call pip is to call the module from the Python that you are using, e.g. instead of 'pip  <args.', say python -m pip <args>'.
5) My original reason for not building the C command-line programs as Python extension modules is outdated. It used to be that every time Python changed a minor version, you had to update the Python headers for the extension module and recompile, and it would then work only with that exact Python version. Cosimo has been working with Ctypes to develop a wrapper for some of the fonttools extensions, that would let you write your functions as regular dynamic libraries, and they could be called by any Python version. I plan to eventually copy this code and make some of the afdko C programs be Python extensions again.

`person = Entity(key=Key('Person'))` confuses me, and makes me go "why didn't they just fold that in...?". Particularly when I'm just starting.

The answer we have (that the JSON we would submit is `{key: { path: [{ kind: 'Person' }] } }`) is really not sufficient IMO. The extra answer (that "Kinds" only exist by virtue of an Entity with a Key of that Kind existing) doesn't help much either given the fact that `index.yaml` let's you define an index on a Kind regardless of whether an entity exists or not, or the future plans to define indexes over an API, or several other places that Kind is expressed as something separate from the Key (in general, the Datastore team tends to describe Kind to outsiders as a 'table/type hybrid thing').

To give some further perspective, in the UI, Kind is treated as a separate concept: you can choose it from a drop-down and browse "only stuff of a certain kind" as well as choose from a drop-down of Kinds when creating a new Entity in the UI. The Key is either "id" or "name", where you can fill in the name. 

Additionally, Kind is treated as a top-level concept in the docs...

I agree that we should take it into consideration that a Kind is really defined on a Key, however our top priority is not 1-1 alignment with the API specification, it's convenience (without being detrimental). I don't think `Entity('Person')` is detrimental.

I did spike on a few changes to support running the 'classic' callback parser support in exp against an existing pants BUILD tree over in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/2312.

For ~apples to apples I ran pants and the exp/commands:list from exploded pexes and found after many trials using each with low variance:
```
$ ./pants binary src/python/pants/bin:pants_local_binary
$ mkdir dist/pants && unzip -qd dist/pants/ dist/pants_local_binary.pex
$ time python2 dist/pants --spec-excludes=$PWD/contrib/go/examples list
1.770

$ ./pants binary src/python/pants/engine/exp/commands:list
$ mkdir dist/list && unzip -qd dist/list/ dist/list.pex
$ time python2 dist/list --spec-excludes=$PWD/contrib/go/examples
1.100
```

The most obvious difference is the avoidance of a majority of callback setup in the exp 'classic' callback parser.  Instead only context awares are made new each parse (~7 for pantsbuild/pants), and the rest of the callbacks are global to all parses and use a global callback accumulator list that is locked, cleared, collected in and copied per-parse.

I have not profiled yet to compare times spent in parsing for a more accurate comparison.

My thought is that continuous variables offer a lot of possible solutions. Efficient code will favor a plugin style. The first attempt could be a robust but simple statistical examination with a cluster of linear approximations providing the solution as a continuous function approximation. For each X(i) in the solution space the associated vector would be used for all x values such that x>=X(i) and x<X(i+1) To extend this into infinite space X(0) might have it's value be ignored, and only the associated function considered. Each function would of course be in the form y=Ax+B. And a convention of A=NaN would return NaN. Thus when X(0) has A=NaN the solution has a lower bound in X(1). X(n) with A=NaN would also provide an upper bound. The only time I can see a small issue is with F(x) blowing up to infinity, where you want a particular discrete value to return NaN. In practice I think an exclusion range around the function is going to be out of practical bounds for almost every case.

After thinking about this for a bit, I don't think API is general enough, and doesn't cover the right abstractions.  At the highest level this is the class that will provide representations of the things necessary for doing deep NLP: tokens, tokens-in-context, sentences, spans, etc.  In addition, implementations of each of those concrete objects has a different assumed input-output tensor shape and we should also specify those shapes as a part of this API.

In addition, the API isn't uniform between the two representations it provides now (`embed_text_input` takes input tensors and returns tensors, but `get_recurrent_layer` takes a name and returns the layer).  We should unify those into one API, where they all either adopt a  "tensor-in-tensor-out" signature, or a `get_*` signature that returns a module the client uses to construct the forward pass.  I suggest the second option as it's more flexible.  

For example, we could have:
```
class NLPRepresentaions:
  def get_token_embedder(self, name):
    # returns a torch.nn.Module that takes Field id input tensors and returns (batch_size, n_tokens, dim) tensor.

  def get_token_in_context_encoder(self, name, from_ids=False):
    # returns a torch.nn.Module that takes a (batch_size, n_tokens, input_dim) of embedded tokens
    # and returns a  (batch_size, n_tokens, output_dim) tensor of encoded tokens in context
    # if from_ids=True then returns a module that takes id inputs and returns encoded tokens-in-context

  def get_sentence_encoder(self, name, from_ids=False):
    # returns torch.nn.Module that takes a (batch_size, n_tokens, input_dim) of embedded tokens
    # and returns a (batch_size, output_dim) tensor of encoded sentences
...
```
This should now be ready for review.

And you're obliged/forced to use `curator_cli` rather than regular Curator?  Because, as you've discovered, there is no way to exit with a 0 exit code with `curator_cli` unless it exited cleanly.

A potential work-around would be to use `show_indices` with the same `--filter_list` _before_ you run the `delete_indices` command.  If no indices show up, then you can skip running that particular command, avoiding a non-zero exit code from the _actual_ call you want to make.  Just don't test for one on the `show_indices` command, either:

```
root@esclient:~# curator_cli  show_indices --filter_list '{"filtertype":"pattern","kind":"prefix","value":"ipmi"}'
ipmi-000002
root@esclient:~# echo $?
0
root@esclient:~# curator_cli  show_indices --filter_list '{"filtertype":"pattern","kind":"prefix","value":"ugly"}'
root@esclient:~# echo $?
1
```

I run into this issue while searching for the very same answer. Hence posting my workaround which may help you/others.

In short, I replaced a couple of lines in the downloaded script to install CLI to read standard input (by default it connects to tty) and then used "here string" to provide input to accept defaults. This seem to have worked as expected. 

[Reference to install Azure CLI 2.0 and its dependencies](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/install-azure-cli#a-namelinuxinstall-on-linux-without-apt-get)

[Here strings reference](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Here_document#Here_strings)

The code to install

```` bash
yum check-update; sudo yum install -y gcc python libffi-devel python-devel openssl-devel
curl -L https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCli -o cli-install.sh
chmod +x ./cli-install.sh
sed -i -e "s/^_TTY/#&/;s/< $_TTY/#&/" ./cli-install.sh
./cli-install.sh <<< "



"
````

`override-client-name: <name>` seems to do the job. The title override seems to work but description still doesn't. I removed the description from the readme which forces Autorest to pull it out of the swagger itself -- that's accurate, at least 😄 

Here's the [readme diff](https://github.com/azure/azure-rest-api-specs/compare/master...alexeldeib:pythonReadme). 

The generated code is identical except for the description [[diff]](https://github.com/alexeldeib/azure-sdk-for-python/compare/loganalytics...alexeldeib:pythonReadmeFix) 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6800857/41382667-0e7bacee-6f22-11e8-8ed4-5277f9a2dc66.png)

So I see three options:
- Keep the global description as "Operational Insights" and the python description as "Log Analytics". When autorest overrides for description work, you'll get an auto PR that fixes the single-line diff. 
- Remove global and Python level descriptions, allowing Autorest to pull from the swagger. Only downside here is the text now says "API", but circumvents the problem entirely. 
- Wait until this is fixed in autorest to merge at all.

Personally, I'd prefer to avoid option 3 -- it doesn't make sense to block on that single line description. I've gone with the second option for right now. If you think that's okay, I'll merge the corresponding readme diff into the spec repo. Or if you prefer the first option, I can make the change here and merge the corresponding readme change as well.

I've begun to question the wisdom of storing `Undefined` objects in `_data`. It might be more straightforward simply to leave out missing values altogether.

The `Undefined` singleton is only needed when we need a placeholder to represent the absence of a value. Notably, the transformation loops and the related functions need to be able to pass around a single value at a time, so `Undefined` is crucial there.

But the main API is not going to expose the placeholders, so why store them? It's easier to turn a missing `_data` item into an `Undefined` on the fly than to prune out the placeholders when they're not wanted.

>Also, do not touch the methods in actions.py. The API server calls them to carry out most of its functionality.

This is actually pretty problematic. They're called by the API server, and then many of the calls in there call back to the API server, causing function call graphs that invoke the call in actions.py twice (cli_register for example), and obfuscate or mangle option defaults. In general, the `args` object doesn't handle default well, and makes it so that if a function call wants to specify say the second optional argument, it has to also specify the first. This isn't good long term. The API server should be invoking methods that look more like methods, and the CLI should interact through the API server. 

I think moving away from the current structure of the actions.py will help with code maintenance a great deal.

Now it's `pytest-aiohttp` the one having this edge case but anyone can do an `asyncio.set_event_loop(None)` in a pytest fixture and then `pytest-asyncio` starts crashing. IMO this should be solved regardless of what other libraries do

So, if you're using the `@pytest.mark.asyncio` marker on a coroutine, this is what generally happens:

* find the event_loop fixture (this is so it can be overridden)
* get the event loop from it (the event loop is what the fixture *is*)
* use the event loop to run the marked coroutine
* close the event loop

If you have a more complex scenario, you don't actually need to use the marker at all. Just make an ordinary pytest test function that receives the event_loop fixture, do whatever you like, and then use the given event loop to run something (`event_loop.run_until_complete()`). Does this help?

Furthermore, pytest fixtures can depend on other fixtures. There's nothing stopping you from doing something like this:

```
def my_fixture(event_loop):
    # Do whatever, including asyncio.ensure_future()

@pytest.mark.asyncio
@pytest.mark.usefixtures('my_fixture')
async def test_something():
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
```

I think this should work (can't test it out currently). It's more complex but if it fits your use case, a little cleaner.

Not sure if this is the proper way but I am putting my solution here in case anybody else runs into this problem. What I ended up doing was taking the suggestion of using `Basket.is_quantity_allowed()` to check the basket totals by passing a line argument:

```python
class Basket(AbstractBasket):
    
    def is_quantity_allowed(self, qty, line=None):
        """Override to check basket level validation.
        
        Add some validation to make sure the same products on different lines
        are not oversold.
        """
        
        #Call the original and stop if there are any problems found
        is_allowed, reason = super(Basket, self).is_quantity_allowed(qty)
        if not is_allowed:
            return is_allowed, reason
            
        #Check the basket totals
        if line:
            is_permitted, reason = line.purchase_info.availability.is_purchase_permitted(self.basket_quantity(line) + qty)
            if not is_permitted:
                reason = _(
                    "{reason} (there may be others in your basket)"
                ).format(
                    reason=reason,
                )
                return False, reason
                
        return True, None
        
    def basket_quantity(self, line):
        """Return the quantity of similar lines in the basket.
        
        The basket can contain multiple lines with the same product and
        stockrecord, but different options. Those quantities are summed up.
        """
        if line.product.is_scheduled:
            matching_lines = self.lines.filter(attributes__option__code=Option.GROUP)
        else:
            matching_lines = self.lines.filter(stockrecord=line.stockrecord)
        quantity = matching_lines.aggregate(Sum('quantity'))['quantity__sum']
        return quantity or 0
```
This has to be called somewhere during the `BasketLineForm` cleaning with the line argument passed:
```python
class BasketLineForm(CoreBasketLineForm):

    def check_max_allowed_quantity(self, qty):
        """Override to pass the line.
        """
        qty_delta = qty - self.instance.quantity
        is_allowed, reason = self.instance.basket.is_quantity_allowed(qty_delta, line=self.instance)
        if not is_allowed:
            raise forms.ValidationError(reason)
```
I also added a similar check to `CheckoutSessionMixin.check_basket_is_valid()`:
```python
def check_basket_is_valid(self, request):
        """Checks to maker sure the basket is still valid. Called at each
        checkout step. 
        
        Add some validation to make sure the same products on different lines
        are not oversold.
        """
        super(CheckoutSessionMixin, self).check_basket_is_valid(request)
        messages = []
        strategy = request.strategy
        
        for line in request.basket.all_lines():
            result = strategy.fetch_for_line(line)
            basket_quantity = request.basket.basket_quantity(line)
            is_permitted, reason = result.availability.is_purchase_permitted(basket_quantity)
            if not is_permitted:
                msg = _(
                    "'{title}' is no longer available to buy (there are {basket_quantity} in your basket - {reason}). "
                    "Please adjust your basket to continue"
                ).format(
                    title=line.product.get_title(),
                    basket_quantity=basket_quantity,
                    reason=reason,
                )
                messages.append(msg)
                
        if messages:
            raise exceptions.FailedPreCondition(url=reverse("basket:summary"), messages=messages)
```
This will make sure the basket is valid at checkout.

At the **[.NET Core Runtime & Host Setup Repo](https://github.com/dotnet/core-setup/blob/master/README.md#daily-builds)** GitHub page we find all supported runtimes.
Mostly x64 that is more precisely the amd64 architecture.
Only the following arm architectures are supported

- Windows (arm32)
- Windows (arm64)
- Linux ([armhf](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ARM_architecture)) or (ARM hard float) refers to the ARMv7 and it is 32 bit, used by Raspberry PI, that is for makers only and not for production use due to quality and scarce availability for large-scale production.

It seems to me that the only **arm64v8** option is **Windows IoT**.

Microsoft does not have official plans yet to support arm64v8 Linux.

Let's Encrypt calls `upload-script`, and pipes the challenges to the standard input, something like: 
example.com challenge-filename-1 challenge-1 
www.example.com challenge-filename-2 challenge-2
> It would be trivial to generalize this with another argument like `--shell-pipe-format` with options such as `csv`, `tsv`, `json`, etc. > 3. > I'll gladly add a test for this, but, out of personal curiosity, when would this ever happen?

I doubt it would, but it's not like it's a guarantee of `time.tzname` that it is encoded in the preferred locale encoding, so we can't rely on that even though it's going to be the case in almost all instances. People probably do just mess with their locale settings until things work and then stop, though, so I wouldn't be surprised if there were a few systems out there like this due to crazy configurations.

> I don't mind decoding on the fly, but that might add some overhead. Is that OK? If not, any suggestions for minimizing the overhead?

I don't really care that much about the overhead in Python 2.7. If you're comfortable using a 10-year-old version of Python, you should probably also be comfortable using a 1-year-old version of dateutil if you're that performance sensitive. The parser itself is also probably not a particularly performant beast, so one extra function call probably won't make a difference.

Something like:

```python
def _get_tznames():
    return time.tzname

if PY2:
   try:
        from tz.tzwin import _get_tznames
    except ImportError:
        pass
```

For the moment, it doesn't really matter what `_get_tznames` returns, since the parser has no ability to recognize Windows-style time zone abbreviations (it is expecting 3- and 4-letter abbreviations). An empty tuple is fine with a comment explaining why, or if you want to be proper there are functions in `tz.tzwin` that will get you at least one of the zone names in a way that respects `tzset()` I believe. I can look into it more carefully this weekend maybe.

> One problem here is that the semantics might be slightly unclear when you pass a naive datetime.

OK, so while testing this, I realized that we're not being hit by that codecov issue. What was happening was that `codecov run -m pytest -v` was inferring that the only source was `dateutil.test` for whatever reason.

By explicitly adding `coverage run --source=dateutil -m pytest -v`, we get the full report.

Unfortunately, this doesn't actually bring up our coverage at all, because the old erroneous reports from Appveyor were counting `dateutil.test` twice, and `dateutil.test` has excellent coverage.

        result = result / (compute_norm(user_repr, no_components) * compute_norm(item_repr, no_components))

    return result
```

```
cdef inline void compute_representation(CSRMatrix features,
                                        flt[:, ::1] feature_embeddings,
                                        flt[::1] feature_biases,
                                        FastLightFM lightfm,
                                        int row_id,
                                        double scale,
                                        flt *representation) nogil:
    """
    Compute latent representation for row_id.
    The last element of the representation is the bias.
    """

    cdef int i, j, start_index, stop_index, feature
    cdef flt feature_weight
    cdef flt norm
    cdef flt *current_emb = <flt *> malloc(lightfm.no_components+1 * sizeof(flt))

    start_index = features.get_row_start(row_id)
    stop_index = features.get_row_end(row_id)

    for i in range(lightfm.no_components + 1):
        representation[i] = 0.0

    for i in range(start_index, stop_index):
        feature = features.indices[i]
        for j in range(lightfm.no_components+1):
            current_emb[j] = feature_embeddings[feature, j]
        norm = 1 / compute_norm(current_emb, lightfm.no_components+1)
        feature_weight = features.data[i] * scale * norm
        for j in range(lightfm.no_components):
            representation[j] += feature_embeddings[feature, j] * feature_weight

        representation[lightfm.no_components] += feature_biases[feature] * feature_weight

    free(current_emb)
```

Indeed, I feel much the same about this. This would have been trivial to sort out at the time IEEE754 was compiled, now it's a bit of a pain. 

There aren't too many operations that suffer from this problem though. Add/sub/muk/div are almost unaffected by this (except when rounding to negative). So, we can always wrap our min/max's in if-then-elses that check for double zeros and return our preferred result, otherwise do the default. This way all different implementations can be handled soundly and it's not more expensive, as internally we'd do exactly the same.

Adding a `bug_is_open` function would provide a partial solution to this problem:

```python
def test_foo(self):
    self.skipIf(bug_is_open(1332))
    self.assertEqual(call_to_server(), 'foo')
```

Of course, each bug will eventually be fixed and marked as "closed". As a result, this solution won't work too well if Pulp Smash is run against an older version of Pulp. The test will run due to the bug report being closed, but the older version of Pulp will still suffer from the issue and fail the bug.

How do we solve that? One solution is to piggy-back on the solution described in https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/issues/29#issuecomment-155087557. We add an optional "version" attribute into the config file, and after a bug has been fixed, we update so it mentions the version in which the bug was fixed:

```python
def test_foo(self):
    self.skipIf(self.cfg.version < Version('2.7.4'))
    self.assertEqual(call_to_server(), 'foo')
```

One weakness of this work-flow is that bugs will be fixed and marked as "closed" in the bug tracker, yet as an end user, the only change I'll see is that a test will change from being skipped to succeeding. As a result, we leave ourselves option to the same workflow issue described above: a bug can be fixed, and when you run Pulp Smash against older versions of Pulp inappropriate tests will execute and fail. One solution to this issue is simply human vigilance: we periodically review the calls to `bug_is_open()` and see if any calls should be updated. A second solution is to make an aggressive version of that function that behaves something like this:

```python
def bug_is_open(bug_id, error_if_closed=True):
    if get_bug_state in OPEN_BUG_STATES:
        return True
    elif error_if_closed:
        raise BugClosedException(
            'This bug is closed. Update the test with something like this: '
            '`self.skipIf(self.cfg.version < Version("1.2.3"))`. Replace '
            '"1.2.3" with the actual version in which the bux is fixed.'
        )
    else:
        return False
```

btw, per @michaelsembwever's comment:

> The end-user does not know, and ultimately should not care, if the span that they are now creating is part of an existing trace or will be initiating a new trace.

This was certainly very true given Dapper's use of threadlocals (which had its ups and downs, admittedly). The caller would basically just create a span blindly, and the implementation (1) looked for an existing parent span/tracecontext in the threadlocal, then (2) created a child span or a root span depending on what was found there.

@michaelsembwever FWIW to a certain extent we are talking about this because some of the initial interfaces were designed in Go where the language itself basically forces you to be totally explicit: no function overloading, no threadlocals, no generics. So @yurishkuro is right, especially in that context: the caller probably *does* know which sort of Span he/she is creating much of the time.

None of the above is meant to argue strongly in either direction. Honestly, I *wish* we could just have one method to construct a Span and let the impls sort it out. I simply don't know how to do that in Go, though(*)... So perhaps this is one of those areas where the per-platform APIs will have to deviate.

(*): without some bullshit `StartSpan(opName string, horribleHack ...interface{})` idiom

This is strange. So running directly from the command line if it is pip installed doesn't name the top level script __ main __? This defeats the whole purpose of the __ name __==" __ main __ " check.

Putting the sys.argv into the main function is a bit of a hack though because it can result in some serious side effects as it basically overwrites the input variables for the main function from a mutable global list variable (sys.argv). 

So there is the scenario where you call Demag GUI and because we have the same flags I overwrite the WD input variable with the -WD flag because it is in sys.argv so demag GUI opens in the original directory not the one Pmag GUI is in now. Or even worse if we use the same flag for different variables we run the risk of name space collisions, and as most command line flags are one character this is a real chance as there are only 26 uniques. That is why python generally uses the __ name __==" __ main __ " solution, this is particularly evident in the conversion scripts which need to be both module and script and have the WD flag in all of them meaning if I solved this by dropping the command line portion into the main function you would only ever be able to run Pmag GUI in the directory you start in if you use the WD flag. 

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

After looking at a bunch of pip setup_tools docs it looks like this is an artefact of setup_tools which actually wraps scripts in another script to run it for "reasons". They recommend solving this using entry points which are look like a bit of a pain. Though I guess this can be solved by having more functions one that reads command line arguments and one that starts the GUI or converts data. i.e for the GUI:

start(**kwargs) : starts the wx.App and GUI
main() : reads command line flags and parses data into kwargs which it passes to start
if __ name __==" __ main __ " : just call main as that is the entry point for pip

The point of the break of these pieces is that if they are together one has to take precedence over the other this way they are separate and Pmag GUI can start Demag GUI without having to worry about what was passed in the console. I'll have to go through and fix this in a number of places though including the conversion scripts and Pmag Dialogs. Thoughts @moonshoes87?

Hi @SMZCC @jdhao. It definitely is possible to download the required scripts manually and store them in the location that visdom expects them. We used to make these packages install during the initial download and setup, but then it was impossible to get visdom when our external content distributors were down. 

[Here's](https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/py/server.py#L538) the function that actually does the collecting. [This line](https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/py/server.py#L609) is the file name that we expect to find the file that we download from the location stored in [key](https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/py/server.py#L611). As long as you download these files and put them in the expected location, the server won't need to attempt to download them externally on runtime.

I think @pschella refers to something a bit more worrisome: that in a class definition such as

```cpp
py::class_<Base>(..)
   .def(py::init<int>());

py::class_<Derived, Base>(..)
   .def(py::init<std::string>());
```

The ``Base(int)`` constructor may be called given the expression ``Derived(1)`` (rather than issuing an error message). Or did I get that wrong?

But passing arguments by reference is a very common idiom in C/C++.  So I think it would be nice if the docs at least said:
a) You can't do it
b) Provided a small example showing how to work around this limitation
Thanks for the report; this should be fixed now.

Yes, we explicitly defer `None` to the converting pass for `char *` arguments, because it isn't obviously a definite match for a `char *`, and setting a `char *` to a nullptr is a little out of the ordinary, so we want to deprioritize it in case there is something else (like a `py::object` or `py::none` argument) that makes more sense as a match.

The other thing happening here, which isn't always immediately obvious, is that defaults specified via `py::arg`s are always Python object, which means a C++ value gets implicitly cast to Python when the default is stored.  In this case, `py::arg("a") = nullptr` is really the same thing as `py::arg("a") = py::cast(nullptr)`, which itself is equivalent to `py::arg("a") = py::none()`.

As for working around it, you can *also* add an overload that takes a `py::none`, then invokes the C++ code as desired with an explicit `nullptr`.  (Though obviously that scales badly if there are several `char *` arguments where you would want `nullptr` values).

Catching this error earlier in Certbot's execution requires checking that ports are available before we know what challenges we need to perform. For example, with the current state of the ACME protocol, Certbot, and Let's Encrypt, if you use the standalone plugin without specifying `--standalone-supported-challenges` or `--preferred-challenges` on the command line, only the `TLS-SNI-01` challenge is performed and we only check that this port is available.

To move this check any earlier than we do now we either need to:

1. Code in assumptions that we can't safely make about the ACME protocol and Let's Encrypt's behavior about what challenges will be performed.
2. Check that every port that is possibly required is available.

We feel that either of these options is worse than what we're currently doing so I'm marking this issue as wontfix.
